The line of thrust at $x = 5$ m can be found from the moment equation: $$H \cdot y = V_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ $$300 \cdot y = 150 \times 5 - \dfrac{30 \times 25}{2}$$ $$300 y = 750 - 375 = 375$$ $$y = 1.25 \text{ m}$$ **Vertical intercept:** $$y' = y_0 - y = 3.75 - 1.25 = 2.50 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 3: Bending Moment by Eddy's Theorem $$M_x = H \cdot y' = 300 \times 2.50 = 750 \text{ kN·m}$$ ### Verification by Direct Method $$M_x = H \cdot y_0 - V_A \cdot x + \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ $$M_x = 300 \times 3.75 - 150 \times 5 + \dfrac{30 \times 25}{2}$$ $$M_x = 1125 - 750 + 375 = 750 \text{ kN·m} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Both methods give the same answer** — confirming Eddy's Theorem: $M = H \cdot y'$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Decision Flow | Step | Question | Action | Output | |------|----------|--------|--------| | 1 | Is the arch symmetric? | Yes → use symmetry; No → compute all reactions | $V_A, V_B$ | | 2 | Is there a crown hinge? | Yes → use $M_C = 0$ to find $H$ | $H$ | | 3 | What is the linear arch shape? | UDL on full span → parabola | $y_0(x)$ | | 4 | What is the actual line of thrust? | Use moment eqn of equilibrium polygon | $y(x)$ | | 5 | Apply Eddy's Theorem | $y' = y_0 - y$ or $y' = y - y_0$ | $y'$ | | 6 | Compute bending moment | $M = H \cdot y'$ | $M_x$ | | 7 | Check sign convention | Tension inside → positive $M$ | Final $M_x$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Eddy's Theorem Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Applied Loads on Arch] --> B{Type of Loading?} B -->|UDL Full Span| C[Linear Arch = Parabola] B -->|UDL Half Span| D[Linear Arch = Two Parabolic Segments] B -->|Point Load| E[Linear Arch = Two Straight Lines + Hinge] C --> F[Compute y0 from Arch Equation] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Find H from Crown Hinge Condition] G --> H[Find Line of Thrust y from Equilibrium] H --> I[Compute Vertical Intercept y prime] I --> J[Apply Eddy: M equals H times y prime] J --> K[Plot BMD] J --> L[Check Middle-Third Rule] J --> M[Compute Fibre Stresses] ``` ## 4.2 Geometric Schematic: Eddy's Theorem Visualized ```mermaid graph LR A[Support A] --> B[Linear Arch: Parabola y0] C[Support B] --> B B --> D[Point P on Linear Arch] E[True Arch: Actual Geometry] --> F[Point Q on True Arch] D -->|Tangent| G[Tangent Line Slope tan alpha] F --> H[Vertical Intercept y prime] D -->|Horizontal Distance| I[x prime] G --> J[Eddy Relation: y prime equals x prime times tan alpha] I --> J J --> K[Bending Moment M equals H times y prime] ``` ## 4.3 Three-Hinged Parabolic Arch — Free Body Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LeftSupport A[Support A
Reaction VA upward
Reaction H inward] end subgraph ArchBody L[Section at distance x
Bending moment Mx

Reaction VB upward

Hinge: MC equals 0
This gives H equals wL squared over 8h] end A -->|wL/2| L L -->|H from both sides| C C -->|wL/2| R R -.->|Returns reaction| A ``` ## 4.4 Force Decomposition at Arch Section ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ResultantForce RF[Resultant Force R at section] end RF -->|Horizontal Component| HF[H = Constant along arch] RF -->|Vertical Component| VF[V = Shear at section] RF -->|Resolved along tangent| AN[Axial Force N = H cos alpha + V sin alpha] HF -->|Moment Arm y prime| BM[Bending Moment M = H times y prime] VF -->|Moment Arm zero| BM ``` ## 4.5 Typical KTU Arch Geometry — Labeled Schematic ```mermaid graph TB S1[Support A: Hinge at x equals 0] -->|Rises to| CR[Crown at x equals L/2, y equals h] CR -->|Falls to| S2[Support B: Hinge at x equals L] S1 -->|Linear Arch Path| AP[Parabolic curve y = 4h x L-x divided by L squared] CR -->|Crown Hinge at top| CH[Allows rotation: M = 0 at C] S1 -.->|Horizontal Thrust H| S2 S1 -.->|Vertical Reaction V sub A| S2 S2 -.->|Vertical Reaction V sub B| S1 AP -->|Vertical Intercept| VI[y prime = y arch - y thrust] VI -->|Eddy Application| EM[Mx = H times y prime] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Legend:** In the schematic above, $x$-axis is horizontal (along the span), $y$-axis is vertical (rise). The linear arch is the **outer parabola**; the equilibrium polygon (line of thrust) is the **inner curve** when the actual loading deviates from full-span UDL. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 KTU Frequently Asked Concepts - Definition of **linear arch** vs **true arch** - Statement of **Eddy's Theorem** (both forms) - Relationship $M = H \cdot y'$ - Bending moment calculation in three-hinged arches - Sign convention for $y'$ and $H$ - Application in **middle-third rule** for masonry arches --- ## 5.2 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### Question A1: Definition of Linear Arch **[KTU University Exam — May 2022]** **Q:** Define *linear arch* (or *theoretical arch*). State Eddy's Theorem in its bending-moment form. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Linear Arch:** A linear arch (also called the theoretical arch or funicular arch) is an arch of the same span and rise as the actual arch, but shaped so that it lies along the **funicular polygon** (equilibrium polygon) of the given system of loads. Under these loads, every section of the linear arch experiences **pure axial compression** with **zero bending moment** and **zero shear**. > > **Eddy's Theorem (Bending Moment Form):** *The bending moment at any section of an arch is equal to the horizontal thrust multiplied by the vertical intercept between the linear arch and the line of thrust at that section.* > > $$M_x = H \cdot y'$$ > > where $H$ = horizontal thrust, $y'$ = vertical intercept. --- ### Question A2: Statement of Eddy's Theorem (Geometrical Form) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2021]** **Q:** State Eddy's Theorem. What is the significance of the *vertical intercept* $y'$ in the theorem? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > **Eddy's Theorem (Geometrical Form):** *The vertical intercept between the linear arch and the equilibrium polygon (line of thrust) at any section is equal to the horizontal distance between them multiplied by the tangent of the angle of inclination of the linear arch at that section.* > > $$y' = x' \tan\alpha$$ > > **Significance of $y'$:** The vertical intercept $y'$ is a **measure of eccentricity** between the line of thrust and the arch axis. It directly determines the **bending moment** via $M = H \cdot y'$. A larger $y'$ means greater bending, and the line of thrust must lie within the **middle-third** of the arch cross-section to avoid tension cracks in masonry construction (middle-third rule). --- ## 5.3 Part B Questions (ESE Module 3 — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Sign convention** errors cause **1–2 marks** loss in most problems. Always state it explicitly. > - Students often forget to include the **self-weight of the arch** in UDL problems (KTU gives $w$ as the total UDL on the horizontal span). > - The **linear arch** must be drawn or its equation must be written before applying Eddy's Theorem. > - The vertical intercept $y'$ must be computed with the **correct sign** (positive if line of thrust is above the linear arch on the loaded side, depending on the convention). > - In **half-span UDL** problems, the linear arch has **two different parabolic segments** — students often wrongly use a single parabola, losing 3–4 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** A three-hinged parabolic arch has a span of $30\text{ m}$ and a central rise of $6\text{ m}$. It carries a uniformly distributed load of $40\text{ kN/m}$ over the **entire span**. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Find the **horizontal thrust**, **vertical reactions**, and **bending moment** at a section $8\text{ m}$ from the left support. **Use Eddy's Theorem** to determine the bending moment. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks If the same arch carries a UDL of $40\text{ kN/m}$ over the **left half only**, determine the **bending moment** at the same section using Eddy's Theorem. Comment on the **sign and magnitude** of the moment compared to part (a). --- #### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a) **Step 1: Geometry** - Span: $L = 30$ m, Rise: $h = 6$ m. - Equation of parabolic arch axis: $y_0 = \dfrac{4h}{L^2} x(L-x) = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{900} x(30-x) = \dfrac{24x(30-x)}{900}$ At $x = 8$ m: $$y_0 = \dfrac{24 \times 8 \times 22}{900} = \dfrac{4224}{900} = 4.693 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Vertical Reactions (UDL over full span)** $$V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{40 \times 30}{2} = 600 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3: Horizontal Thrust** For a three-hinged parabolic arch with UDL on the full span, the bending moment at the crown is zero. Taking moments about the crown from the right side: $$H \cdot h = V_B \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$ $$H = \dfrac{600 \times 15}{6} = 1500 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4: Apply Eddy's Theorem** Since the UDL is on the **full span**, the **linear arch is a parabola** and **coincides with the actual arch axis**. Therefore the **line of thrust also lies along the parabola** (it is the funicular for full-span UDL). $$y' = y_0 - y = 0 \text{ (line of thrust coincides with linear arch)}$$ **Step 5: Bending Moment** $$M_x = H \cdot y' = 1500 \times 0 = 0$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** For a **three-hinged parabolic arch** with **UDL on the full span**, the bending moment is **zero everywhere** because the arch geometry matches the funicular polygon. This is why parabolic arches are preferred for UDL loading. --- #### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b) **Step 1: UDL on Left Half** Loading: $w = 40$ kN/m on the left half ($0 \le x \le 15$ m). Total UDL on left half = $wL/2 = 40 \times 15 = 600$ kN, acting at $x = 7.5$ m. **Step 2: Vertical Reactions** By symmetry of the structure (not the load) and the hinge at the crown: $$V_A = V_B = \dfrac{600}{2} = 300 \text{ kN}$$ (Verification: $V_A + V_B = 600$ kN $\checkmark$) **Step 3: Horizontal Thrust** Taking moments about the crown from the right side (no load on right half): $$H \cdot h = V_B \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = 300 \times 15 = 4500$$ $$H = \dfrac{4500}{6} = 750 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4: Linear Arch (Parabola)** The **linear arch** for the given load is *not* the same as the **actual arch axis** (which is still the parabola of span 30 m and rise 6 m, since the geometry is fixed). The linear arch for the half-span UDL is a **two-segment polygon** that we use to find the line of thrust. The **line of thrust** (equilibrium polygon) at $x = 8$ m (within the loaded zone): $$H \cdot y = V_A \cdot x - \dfrac{w x^2}{2}$$ $$750 \cdot y = 300 \times 8 - \dfrac{40 \times 64}{2}$$ $$750 y = 2400 - 1280 = 1120$$ $$y = 1.493 \text{ m}$$ **Step 5: Vertical Intercept (Eddy's Theorem)** $$y' = y_0 - y = 4.693 - 1.493 = 3.20 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6: Bending Moment** $$M_x = H \cdot y' = 750 \times 3.20 = 2400 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 7: Comparison with Part (a)** | Quantity | Part (a) Full UDL | Part (b) Half UDL | |----------|-------------------|-------------------| | Horizontal thrust $H$ | 1500 kN | 750 kN | | Vertical intercept $y'$ | 0 m | 3.20 m | | Bending moment $M_x$ | 0 kN·m | 2400 kN·m | > **Conclusion:** In part (b), the moment is **2400 kN·m** at the section. This is a large value, showing that **partial UDL causes significant bending** because the arch geometry no longer matches the funicular for the load. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]** A three-hinged circular arch has a span of $24\text{ m}$ and a central rise of $4\text{ m}$. A concentrated load of $120\text{ kN}$ acts at a distance of $6\text{ m}$ from the left support. #### Part (a) — 7 Marks Determine the **horizontal thrust** and **vertical reactions**. Draw the **linear arch** for this loading. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks Using **Eddy's Theorem**, find the **bending moment** at a section $6\text{ m}$ from the left support (i.e., directly under the load). Comment on the sign. --- #### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a) **Step 1: Geometry — Three-hinged Circular Arch** Span $L = 24$ m, Rise $h = 4$ m. Radius of the circular arch: $$R = \dfrac{L^2 + 4h^2}{8h} = \dfrac{576 + 64}{32} = 20 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2: Vertical Reactions** For a point load $P = 120$ kN at $a = 6$ m from left support: $$V_A = \dfrac{P(L-a)}{L} = \dfrac{120 \times 18}{24} = 90 \text{ kN}$$ $$V_B = \dfrac{P \cdot a}{L} = \dfrac{120 \times 6}{24} = 30 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3: Horizontal Thrust** Taking moments about the crown hinge (at $x = L/2 = 12$ m, $y = h = 4$ m) from the left side: $$H \cdot h = V_A \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} - P \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2} - a\right)$$ $$H \cdot 4 = 90 \times 12 - 120 \times 6$$ $$4H = 1080 - 720 = 360$$ $$H = 90 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4: Linear Arch for a Point Load** For a point load on a three-hinged arch, the **linear arch** consists of **two straight lines** (or more precisely, two segments meeting at a hinge point directly below the load). The funicular polygon for a single point load is a **bending line** with the hinge at the point of application of the load (in the inverted-cable sense). For our problem with the load at $a = 6$ m, the **linear arch** is composed of: - A straight line from $A(0, 0)$ to a kink at $(6, y_k)$ - A straight line from the kink to $B(24, 0)$ passing through the crown $(12, 4)$. Slope of right line: $\tan\theta_2 = \dfrac{4 - 0}{12 - 6} = \dfrac{4}{6}$ — wait, the right line must go from kink to $B$ via crown. Slope from $(12, 4)$ to $(24, 0)$: $\tan\theta_2 = \dfrac{0-4}{24-12} = -\dfrac{1}{3}$ Equation: $y = 4 - \dfrac{1}{3}(x - 12) = 8 - \dfrac{x}{3}$ At $x = 6$ (kink location): $y_k = 8 - 2 = 6$ m. Slope of left line: from $(0,0)$ to $(6, 6)$: $\tan\theta_1 = 1$. Equation of left line: $y = x$ (for $0 \le x \le 6$). The **linear arch** is therefore: $$y_{\text{linear}} = \begin{cases} x, & 0 \le x \le 6 \\ 8 - x/3, & 6 \le x \le 24 \end{cases}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b) **Step 1: Arch Axis at $x = 6$ m** For the **circular arch** at $x = 6$ m: The center of the circle is at $(L/2, R - h) = (12, 16)$. Equation of the arch: $(x - 12)^2 + (y - 16)^2 = 20^2 = 400$ At $x = 6$: $(6-12)^2 + (y-16)^2 = 400$ $$36 + (y-16)^2 = 400 \Rightarrow (y-16)^2 = 364 \Rightarrow y - 16 = \pm 19.08$$ Taking the upper part (arch is above the springing): $y = 16 - 19.08 = -3.08$ — this is **below** the support, which is wrong. Re-evaluating: The springing is at $y = 0$ and crown at $y = 4$. So the center is at $(12, R-h) = (12, 16)$ — but this is *above* the crown. Let me re-derive. For a circular arch, the center is at $y_c = R - h$ below the crown. With $R = 20$ and $h = 4$, $y_c = 16$ above the springing. The arch is a circular arc from $A(0,0)$ to $B(24, 0)$ passing through crown $(12, 4)$. At $x = 6$: $$(6-12)^2 + (y - 16)^2 = 400$$ $$36 + (y-16)^2 = 400 \Rightarrow y = 16 - \sqrt{364} = 16 - 19.08 = -3.08$$ This is incorrect because the arch is a **minor arc** (small rise compared to span). Let me reconsider. The arc is the part of the circle *above* the springing line. At $x = 12$, $y = 4$ (crown). At $x = 0$ and $x = 24$, $y = 0$. The arc is between these three points. The center is at $(12, 16)$, and the radius is 20. The arch is the **lower portion** of the circle (below the center). For a point on the lower arc at $x = 6$: $$(6-12)^2 + (y-16)^2 = 400$$ $$y = 16 - \sqrt{400 - 36} = 16 - \sqrt{364} = 16 - 19.08 = -3.08$$ This is below the springing, which is wrong. The issue is that the center should be **above** the springing for a small-rise arch, and the arc is the part of the circle **below** the center and **above** the springing. Let me recompute: The arc is the part of the circle where $y$ ranges from $0$ to $4$. At $x = 6$: $$(6-12)^2 + (y-16)^2 = 400$$ $$(y-16)^2 = 400 - 36 = 364$$ $$y = 16 \pm 19.08$$ The two solutions are $y = 35.08$ and $y = -3.08$. Neither is in the range $[0, 4]$! This indicates I have an error. Let me recompute $R$. For a circular arch with span $L$ and rise $h$: $$R = \dfrac{L^2}{8h} + \dfrac{h}{2} = \dfrac{576}{32} + 2 = 18 + 2 = 20 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$ The center is at $(L/2, R - h) = (12, 20 - 4) = (12, 16)$. Hmm, the issue is that the chord (springing line) is at $y = 0$ and the center is at $y = 16$, so the circle has $y$ ranging from $16 - 20 = -4$ to $16 + 20 = 36$. The arch (above the springing) has $y$ from $0$ to $4$. So at $x = 6$: $$(6-12)^2 + (y-16)^2 = 400$$ $$(y-16)^2 = 364 \Rightarrow y - 16 = \pm 19.08$$ For the **upper** part of the arch (closer to the center, going from springing to crown): $y = 16 - 19.08 = -3.08$ — this is **below the springing**, but it could be the **other** intersection of the vertical line $x = 6$ with the circle (i.e., the lower half of the circle). The other solution: $y = 16 + 19.08 = 35.08$ — this is way above. Wait, this means the vertical line $x = 6$ does **not** intersect the arch (the part of the circle above the springing line). But this is impossible because the arch spans from $(0,0)$ to $(24,0)$ continuously. Let me reconsider. The issue is that the arch is the **arc** of the circle, not the full circle. At $x = 6$, the arch is between the springing ($y = 0$) and the crown ($y = 4$). Recompute: At $x = 6$, distance from center $(12, 16)$ is: $$\sqrt{(6-12)^2 + (y-16)^2} = \sqrt{36 + (y-16)^2} = 20$$ $$36 + (y-16)^2 = 400$$ $$(y-16)^2 = 364$$ $$y = 16 \pm \sqrt{364} = 16 \pm 19.08$$ So $y = 35.08$ or $y = -3.08$. The arc above the springing is the part of the circle between $(0,0)$, $(12,4)$, and $(24,0)$. At $x = 6$, we need $y$ in $[0, 4]$. But neither solution is in this range! This is mathematically inconsistent. Let me re-examine the geometry. Oh! I see the issue. For a **segmental** arch with $L = 24$ and $h = 4$, the **central rise** $h$ is small, and the **sagitta** (rise) is only 4 m. The circle that fits this is a **large circle** of radius 20 m. The arch is the **minor arc** (shorter arc) of this circle. At $x = 0$, $y = 0$. At $x = 12$, $y = 4$. At $x = 24$, $y = 0$. So the **chord** (springing line) is from $(0,0)$ to $(24,0)$, length 24. The **sagitta** (maximum height) is 4. The center is at perpendicular distance from the chord: $$d = R - h = 20 - 4 = 16$$ But wait, the center can be on either side. For a **circular arch opening upward** (the typical case), the center is **above** the springing line at $(12, 16)$, and the arch is the **lower arc** (the arc that is on the **opposite side** of the chord from the center). Hmm, let me think again. The center is at $(12, 16)$ which is **above** the springing. The circle of radius 20 passes through $(0,0)$, $(12,4)$, and $(24,0)$. The arch is the **part of the circle from $(0,0)$ to $(24,0)$ that is closest to the chord (i.e., the lower part of the circle below the center)**. For the lower part: at $x = 12$, $y = 16 - 20 = -4$ (lowest point of circle), but the arch has $y = 4$ at the crown! This is a contradiction. **Resolution:** The center is actually **below** the springing line for a typical arch. Let me redo: If the center is at $(12, -16)$, the radius is 20, then the upper part of the circle is the arch. At $x = 12$: $y = -16 + 20 = 4$ $\checkmark$ (crown) At $x = 0$: $y = -16 + \sqrt{400 - 144} = -16 + \sqrt{256} = -16 + 16 = 0$ $\checkmark$ (springing) So the center is at $(12, -16)$ (below the springing line). The arch is the **upper arc**. At $x = 6$: $$(6-12)^2 + (y+16)^2 = 400$$ $$36 + (y+16)^2 = 400$$ $$(y+16)^2 = 364$$ $$y = -16 + \sqrt{364} = -16 + 19.08 = 3.08 \text{ m}$$ So the arch axis ordinate at $x = 6$ m is $y_0 = 3.08$ m. **Step 2: Slope of the Arch Axis at $x = 6$ m** Implicit differentiation of $(x-12)^2 + (y+16)^2 = 400$: $$2(x-12) + 2(y+16)\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 0$$ $$\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -\dfrac{x-12}{y+16} = \tan\alpha$$ At $x = 6$, $y = 3.08$: $$\tan\alpha = -\dfrac{6-12}{3.08+16} = \dfrac{6}{19.08} = 0.3144$$ **Step 3: Linear Arch (Funicular Polygon) for the Point Load** From Part (a), the linear arch is: $$y_{\text{linear}} = \begin{cases} x, & 0 \le x \le 6 \\ 8 - x/3, & 6 \le x \le 24 \end{cases}$$ At $x = 6$ (just at the kink): $y_{\text{linear}} = 6$ m (from the left equation) and $= 6$ m (from the right equation, $8 - 6/3 = 6$). Consistent. **Step 4: Vertical Intercept (Eddy's Theorem)** The line of thrust (which is the linear arch for a single point load) at $x = 6$ m is at $y = 6$ m. The arch axis is at $y_0 = 3.08$ m. $$y' = y_{\text{linear}} - y_0 = 6 - 3.08 = 2.92 \text{ m}$$ > **Sign:** The line of thrust is **above** the arch axis, so $y'$ is **positive**. **Step 5: Bending Moment by Eddy's Theorem** $$M_x = H \cdot y' = 90 \times 2.92 = 262.8 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 6: Sign** Since the line of thrust is **above** the arch axis, the moment causes **tension on the inside (concave/lower) face** of the arch. This is the **positive** sign by the KTU convention. $$\boxed{M_{x=6} = +262.8 \text{ kN·m (tension on inside face)}}$$ --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready):** ### Core Concepts - [x] **Linear arch** = arch shaped along the **funicular polygon** of the load → carries load in **pure compression, $M = 0$ everywhere**. - [x] **True arch** = actual structural arch (geometry may differ from funicular). - [x] **Eddy's Theorem (Geometric):** $y' = x' \tan\alpha$. - [x] **Eddy's Theorem (Moment):** $M = H \cdot y'$. ### Standard Formulas - [x] Parabolic arch axis: $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^2} x(L-x)$. - [x] Three-hinged arch horizontal thrust (full UDL): $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$. - [x] Three-hinged arch vertical reactions (UDL): $V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$. - [x] Three-hinged arch horizontal thrust (point load $P$ at distance $a$): $H = \dfrac{P \cdot a \cdot (L-a)}{L \cdot h}$ — derived from $H \cdot h = V_B \cdot L/2$. - [x] Circular arch radius: $R = \dfrac{L^2 + 4h^2}{8h}$. ### Sign Conventions - [x] $H$ always **positive** (inward thrust at supports). - [x] $y'$ **positive** if line of thrust is **above** the arch axis on the section. - [x] $M$ **positive** if tension is on the **inside (concave)** face of the arch. - [x] Slope $\tan\alpha$ is **positive** in the first half (left to crown) and **negative** in the second half. ### Critical Pitfalls - [x] For a **three-hinged parabolic arch with full-span UDL**, $M = 0$ **everywhere** (key trick question!). - [x] For **partial UDL**, the linear arch is **different from the parabolic arch axis** — must compute $y'$ carefully. - [x] For a **circular arch**, the linear arch is a **parabola** (for UDL) or **polygon** (for point loads), **not** the circular arc. - [x] Always check **units**: $H$ in kN, $y'$ in m, $M$ in kN·m. - [x] The **middle-third rule** (no tension in masonry) requires the line of thrust to be within $d/6$ of the axis, where $d$ is the arch thickness. ### Quick Trick - [x] For a three-hinged arch with a **point load at the crown**: $H = 0$ (no horizontal thrust). - [x] For a three-hinged arch with a **point load at the springing**: $H$ is the **maximum** for the given geometry.

topics.title: Analysis of Three-Hinged Arches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT303 - ANALYSIS OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Three-Hinged Arches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Hinged Arch (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **three-hinged arch** is a curved structural member subjected to predominantly axial compression, supported at its two ends by hinges (pin supports) and provided with a third internal hinge — called the **crown hinge** — located typically at the apex of the arch. This configuration renders the arch **statically determinate** to the **first degree of indeterminacy** (Degree of Static Indeterminacy, **DSI = 0**), because a parabolic (or circular) arch is normally once internally indeterminate, and the crown hinge removes that single redundancy.

For a three-hinged parabolic arch of **span L** and **central rise h**, the governing Cartesian equation of the centerline is:

$$y = \frac{4h}{L^{2}} \, x \, (L - x)$$

This is the **second-degree parabola**, symmetric about the crown at $x = L/2$, where $y = h$.

### 1.1.1 Degree of Static Indeterminacy (DSI) Check

| Component | DSI Contribution |
|---|---|
| General curved beam (2-D) | 3 |
| Internal hinge (at crown) | −1 |
| External reactions (4 unknown: $V_A, H_A, V_B, H_B$) | Equations available: 3 equilibrium + 1 condition (ΣM at crown = 0) = 4 |
| **Net DSI** | **0 (Statically Determinate)** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Coconut Leaf Bridge (Kerala Style):**
> Imagine the traditional curved **Kerala-style footbridges** built across small streams using coconut palm midribs bent into an arch. The two ends are tied into the soil (the **abutment hinges**), and the two halves are lashed together at the top with rope (the **crown hinge**). When a person walks across the middle, the rope at the top allows each half of the bridge to rotate slightly — transferring the load primarily as **axial compression** down into the supports rather than as bending.
>
> Just like that rope joint, the crown hinge in a three-hinged arch allows the left half and right half to rotate independently under asymmetric loads, which is precisely why the structure can be analyzed using only **statics** (no compatibility equations needed) — the arch is **statically determinate**.

### 1.2.1 Why Use Arches Instead of Straight Beams?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Advantage:**
> In a simply-supported beam, the load is resisted almost entirely by **bending moment**. In an arch of the same span, the load is resolved into:
> - **Vertical reactions** $V_A$, $V_B$ (at the abutments), and
> - **Horizontal thrust** $H$ (the famous "arch action").
>
> This horizontal thrust produces a **negative bending moment** that largely cancels the positive moment from gravity loads. The net **bending moment** in an arch is therefore drastically smaller than in an equivalent straight beam, and the cross-section is subjected mainly to **axial compression**. This is why arches can be built in **masonry, stone, or unreinforced concrete** — materials that are weak in tension but strong in compression.

### 1.2.2 Standard KTU Notation and Units

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $L$ | Effective span (horizontal distance between abutments) | m |
| $h$ | Central rise (vertical height of crown above springing) | m |
| $V_A, V_B$ | Vertical reactions at left/right supports | kN |
| $H_A, H_B$ | Horizontal reactions at left/right supports | kN |
| $H$ | Horizontal thrust ($H_A = H_B = H$ for most loadings) | kN |
| $M_x$ | Bending moment at section x (distance from left support) | kN·m |
| $N$ | Normal thrust (axial compression) at section | kN |
| $S$ | Radial (tangential) shear at section | kN |
| $\theta$ | Slope of arch tangent at section (measured from horizontal) | radians |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions for KTU-Level Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus treats the three-hinged arch under the following idealizations:
> 1. **Plane geometry**: All loads and geometry lie in a single 2-D plane.
> 2. **Pin/hinge supports**: Both abutments are frictionless hinges ($H \neq 0$).
> 3. **Crown hinge is frictionless**: It transmits force but not moment ($M_{\text{crown}} = 0$).
> 4. **Self-weight neglected** unless explicitly stated (most problems give this assumption).
> 5. **Span = L** measured along the horizontal between the two support hinges.
> 6. **Parabolic profile** unless otherwise specified (circular and segmental arches appear only as minor variants).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Reaction Determination

The three-hinged arch is a "**two-rigid-body system joined at the crown**." We exploit this directly:

**Step 1 — Apply global equilibrium to the whole arch:**
- $\Sigma F_x = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; H_A = H_B = H$ (assuming no horizontal applied loads).
- $\Sigma F_y = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V_A + V_B = \Sigma (\text{vertical applied loads})$.
- $\Sigma M_A = 0$ (taking moments about left support) $\;\Rightarrow\; V_B$.

**Step 2 — Apply the crown-hinge condition:**
- $\Sigma M_{\text{crown}}^{\text{left half}} = 0$ (moments of all forces on the left half, taken about the crown, must vanish).
- This single additional equation solves the **horizontal thrust $H$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this works (the "Why" behind the logic):**
> Without the crown hinge, a curved beam is 1° statically indeterminate. The crown hinge supplies one extra equation ($M = 0$ at the crown), exactly enough to release the indeterminacy and reduce the system to a **statically determinate** one. The crown is therefore a **physical "condition equation generator."**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Reactions for Standard Loadings on a Parabolic Three-Hinged Arch (Span L, Rise h)

| Loading Type | $V_A$ | $V_B$ | Horizontal Thrust $H$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point load $W$ at $x = a$ from A | $\dfrac{W(L-a)}{L}$ | $\dfrac{Wa}{L}$ | $\dfrac{W \cdot a(L-a)}{L \cdot h}$ |
| UDL $w$ over **entire** span | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{2}$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{8h}$ |
| UDL $w$ over **left half** ($0 \le x \le L/2$) | $\dfrac{3wL}{8}$ | $\dfrac{wL}{8}$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{16h}$ |
| UDL $w$ over **right half** ($L/2 \le x \le L$) | $\dfrac{wL}{8}$ | $\dfrac{3wL}{8}$ | $\dfrac{wL^{2}}{16h}$ |
| UDL $w$ over part length $b$ starting at distance $a$ from A | $\dfrac{wb(L - a - b/2)}{L}$ | $V_A + wb$ | $\dfrac{wb \cdot \bar{x}_1 (L - \bar{x}_1)}{L \cdot h}$ where $\bar{x}_1 = a + b/2$ |
| Uniform temperature rise $T$ (with $\alpha$ = coefficient, $I/A$ for arch ring) | 0 | 0 | $\dfrac{\alpha T L}{\int \frac{y \, ds}{EI}}$ |
| Settlement of support | Re-distributed | Re-distributed | Depends on settlement magnitude |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Moment Equation at Section x (left of crown):**
> $$M_x = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y - (\text{moment of applied loads to the left of section, taken about section})$$
> **Universal Shear/Thrust Equations at Section x:**
> $$N = (V_A - V_{\text{load,left}}) \sin\theta + H \cos\theta$$
> $$S = (V_A - V_{\text{load,left}}) \cos\theta - H \sin\theta$$
> where $\tan\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}(L - 2x)$.

### 2.3.2 Geometric / Trigonometric Identities for the Parabola

$$y = \frac{4h}{L^{2}} x (L - x)$$

$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4h}{L^{2}} (L - 2x) = \tan\theta$$

$$\cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}}}, \qquad \sin\theta = \frac{\frac{dy}{dx}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{dy}{dx}\right)^{2}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students forget that **$H$ is constant along the entire arch** (for vertical-load-only cases). Once computed at the crown, the same $H$ acts at every cross-section. This is a direct consequence of $\Sigma F_x = 0$ on the half-arch.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why a Three-Hinged Arch? |
|---|---|
| **Bridge decks (short to medium span)** | Statically determinate → no stresses from support settlement, no temperature stresses for symmetric spans. |
| **Industrial shed roof trusses** | Long clear span without intermediate columns; economical steel sections due to dominant axial compression. |
| **Dam spillway arches** | Transfers hydrostatic load to abutments largely as compression; masonry concrete performs optimally. |
| **Auditorium / sports hall roofs** | Aesthetic curved profile; large unobstructed interior. |
| **Tunnels and culverts** | Resists overburden pressure as ring compression. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Horizontal Thrust for a Concentrated Point Load

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Parabolic three-hinged arch, span $L$, rise $h$. A point load $W$ acts vertically at horizontal distance $a$ from the left support A. We seek $V_A, V_B, H$.

### Step 1 — Global Vertical Equilibrium

The total vertical load is $W$ acting downward. So:

$$V_A + V_B = W \tag{1}$$

### Step 2 — Moment About Left Support (Whole Arch)

Taking moments about A (counterclockwise positive), the load $W$ at distance $a$ produces a clockwise moment $W \cdot a$:

$$V_B \cdot L - W \cdot a = 0$$

$$\boxed{V_B = \frac{Wa}{L}} \tag{2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Back into Equation (1)

$$V_A = W - V_B = W - \frac{Wa}{L}$$

$$\boxed{V_A = \frac{W(L-a)}{L}} \tag{3}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Crown-Hinge Condition (the "Extra Equation")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical step:** Consider only the **left half** of the arch (from A to crown C). Take moments of all forces on this left half **about the crown C**. The crown hinge can transmit force but **no moment**, so the moment of all forces on the left half about C must equal zero.

Coordinates of the crown: $x_C = L/2$, $y_C = h$.

Forces on the left half:
- $V_A$ acting upward at A (horizontal distance from C is $L/2$).
- $H$ acting horizontally inward at A (vertical distance from C is $h$).
- Load $W$ acting **only if** $a \le L/2$ (i.e., the load is on the left half).

**Case A: Load on the left half ($a \le L/2$)**

Taking moments about C, with the convention that $V_A$ tends to rotate the half-arch counterclockwise (positive) and $H$ tends to rotate it clockwise (negative):

$$V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} - a\right) = 0$$

Solving for $H$:

$$H = \frac{V_A \cdot (L/2) - W(L/2 - a)}{h}$$

Substituting $V_A = W(L-a)/L$:

$$H = \frac{\frac{W(L-a)}{L} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - W\left(\frac{L-2a}{2}\right)}{h}$$

$$H = \frac{\frac{W(L-a)}{2} - \frac{W(L-2a)}{2}}{h}$$

$$H = \frac{W}{2h}\left[(L-a) - (L-2a)\right] = \frac{W}{2h}\left[a\right]$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{Wa(L-a)}{Lh}} \tag{4}$$

**Case B: Load on the right half ($a \ge L/2$)**

By symmetry of the analysis (now considering the right half), the same formula applies:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{Wa(L-a)}{Lh}} \tag{4, repeated)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The expression $H = \dfrac{Wa(L-a)}{Lh}$ is **universal** for a concentrated vertical load on a parabolic three-hinged arch, regardless of which half the load lies on.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Horizontal Thrust for UDL over the Entire Span

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Parabolic arch, span $L$, rise $h$. A uniformly distributed load of intensity $w$ (kN/m) acts over the entire horizontal projection.

### Step 1 — Vertical Reactions

By symmetry of geometry and loading, $V_A = V_B$. Total vertical load = $wL$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_A = V_B = \frac{wL}{2}} \tag{5}$$

### Step 2 — Crown Hinge Condition

Consider the left half. The UDL on the left half has total value $w \cdot (L/2)$ and acts at the centroid, which is at horizontal distance $(L/4)$ from A, i.e., at $(L/2 - L/4) = L/4$ from the crown.

Taking moments about the crown C (left half):

$$V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - \left(\frac{wL}{2}\right) \cdot \frac{L}{4} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for H

$$H \cdot h = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{4}$$

$$H \cdot h = \frac{wL^{2}}{4} - \frac{wL^{2}}{8} = \frac{wL^{2}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{wL^{2}}{8h}} \tag{6}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Famous "Arch Thrust" Formula:** Equation (6) is the most-cited result in arch analysis. Note that $H$ is **inversely proportional to the rise $h$** — a flat arch carries a much larger horizontal thrust than a tall one. This is a critical design consideration for the abutments.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bending Moment, Radial Shear, Normal Thrust at a General Section

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** Section is at horizontal distance $x$ from A (left support), with $0 \le x \le L/2$ (left half). Geometry: $y = \frac{4h}{L^{2}} x(L-x)$, $\tan\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$.

### 3.3.1 Total Vertical Force to the Left of Section

Let $W_L$ = total vertical load between A and the section. The "effective" vertical reaction at the section is:

$$V_{\text{eff}} = V_A - W_L$$

### 3.3.2 Bending Moment at Section

Summing moments of all forces to the **left** of the section about the section itself:

$$M_x = V_{\text{eff}} \cdot 0 + V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y - (\text{moment of }W_L \text{ about section})$$

The moment of $W_L$ about the section is $\sum W_i (x - x_i)$ where $x_i$ are the load positions. So generically:

$$\boxed{M_x = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y - \int_{0}^{x} w(\xi)(x - \xi)\,d\xi} \tag{7}$$

For a **point load $W$ at $a$** with section to the left of the load ($x < a$):

$$M_x = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y = \frac{W(L-a)}{L}x - \frac{Wa(L-a)}{Lh}\cdot\frac{4hx(L-x)}{L^{2}}$$

Simplifying term by term:

$$M_x = \frac{W(L-a)}{L}x - \frac{4W a(L-a)}{L^{3}} \cdot x(L-x)$$

$$\boxed{M_x = \frac{W(L-a)}{L}\left[x - \frac{4a}{L^{2}}x(L-x)\right]} \tag{8}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification at crown ($x = L/2$, $a \le L/2$):**
> $M_{L/2} = \dfrac{W(L-a)}{L}\left[\dfrac{L}{2} - \dfrac{4a}{L^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}\right] = \dfrac{W(L-a)}{L}\left[\dfrac{L}{2} - a\right] = \dfrac{W(L-a)(L-2a)}{2L}$. For a load at the crown ($a = L/2$), this gives $M_{L/2} = 0$ — consistent with the crown-hinge condition. ✓

### 3.3.3 Normal Thrust and Radial Shear

The resultant of $V_{\text{eff}}$ and $H$ acting at the section is resolved into components along the arch tangent (normal thrust $N$, acting **along** the arch axis) and perpendicular to it (radial shear $S$).

If $\theta$ is the angle the tangent makes with the horizontal:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= V_{\text{eff}} \sin\theta + H \cos\theta \\
S &= V_{\text{eff}} \cos\theta - H \sin\theta
\end{aligned} \tag{9}$$

with:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\tan\theta}{\sqrt{1+\tan^{2}\theta}}, \qquad \cos\theta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+\tan^{2}\theta}}$$

and $\tan\theta = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}(L - 2x)$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effect of Temperature Rise

> [!NOTE]
> A uniform temperature rise $T^{\circ}\text{C}$ causes a horizontal displacement of the crown relative to the supports if the arch were free to expand. Because the supports restrain this, an **internal horizontal thrust $H_t$** develops. Since the arch is statically determinate for vertical loads, $H_t$ is found from a **compatibility (deflection) condition**.

### Step 3.4.1 — Free Thermal Expansion

If the arch crown were free to move horizontally, the crown hinge would displace by:

$$\delta_{t,\text{free}} = \alpha T L$$

where $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion.

### Step 3.4.2 — Deflection Due to a Unit Horizontal Load at Crown

Applying a unit horizontal force at the crown (in the redundant direction), the horizontal deflection of the crown is:

$$\delta_{H=1} = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{y^{2}}{EI}\,dx$$

For a parabola, $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}x(L-x)$, so $y^{2} = \dfrac{16h^{2}}{L^{4}} x^{2}(L-x)^{2}$.

### Step 3.4.3 — Compatibility Equation

The total horizontal displacement of the crown must equal zero (supports are immovable in the standard problem):

$$\alpha T L - H_t \int_{0}^{L} \frac{y^{2}}{EI}\,dx = 0$$

$$\boxed{H_t = \frac{\alpha T L}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{L} \frac{y^{2}}{EI}\,dx}} \tag{10}$$

For uniform $EI$ and parabolic $y$:

$$\int_{0}^{L} y^{2}\,dx = \int_{0}^{L}\frac{16h^{2}}{L^{4}}x^{2}(L-x)^{2}\,dx = \frac{8 h^{2} L}{15}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{H_t = \frac{15 \alpha T \, EI}{8 h^{2}}} \tag{11}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Error:** Students often write $H_t = \frac{\alpha T L}{h}$, confusing the thermal arch with a beam expansion. The **correct** form involves the integral of $y^{2}/EI$ along the entire arch.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Effect of Support Settlement

If the right support B settles vertically by an amount $\delta$, the arch rotates about A. By geometry, the crown descends by approximately $\delta/2$ (for a symmetric parabolic arch), and the springing line tilts by an angle $\delta/L$. This produces an additional horizontal thrust:

$$H_s = \frac{15 \, EI \, \delta}{2 L h^{2}} \quad \text{(settlement, parabolic arch, uniform EI)} \tag{12}$$

(Reference for derivation: standard KTU textbook treatment of "settlements of supports.")

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Load-Transfer Architecture of a Three-Hinged Arch

The following diagram maps the **load path** through the arch — from the applied load all the way to the ground — which is the primary conceptual model tested in KTU examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LoadA["Applied Vertical Load W or UDL w"]:::loadNode
    CrownH["Crown Hinge C - Transmits Force - No Moment"]:::hingeNode
    LeftHalf["Left Half of Arch - Rigid Curved Member"]:::memberNode
    RightHalf["Right Half of Arch - Rigid Curved Member"]:::memberNode
    SupA["Left Hinged Support A - Reactions VA and HA"]:::supportNode
    SupB["Right Hinged Support B - Reactions VB and HB"]:::supportNode
    ReactionA["Vertical VA equals W times L minus a over L"]:::reactionNode
    ReactionB["Vertical VB equals W times a over L"]:::reactionNode
    ThrustH["Horizontal Thrust H equals W times a times L minus a over L times h"]:::thrustNode
    Compression["Axial Compression N travels through Arch Ring"]:::compressionNode
    Abutment["Massive Abutments Resist Horizontal Thrust"]:::abutmentNode

    LoadA --> CrownH
    CrownH --> LeftHalf
    CrownH --> RightHalf
    LeftHalf --> SupA
    RightHalf --> SupB
    SupA --> ReactionA
    SupB --> ReactionB
    SupA --> ThrustH
    SupB --> ThrustH
    LeftHalf --> Compression
    RightHalf --> Compression
    Compression --> Abutment
    ThrustH --> Abutment

    classDef loadNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef hingeNode fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef memberNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef supportNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef reactionNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef thrustNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef compressionNode fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#2F4F4F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef abutmentNode fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Sectional Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SecInput["Section at horizontal distance x from A"]:::secNode
    CoordY["Compute y equals 4h over L squared times x times L minus x"]:::geomNode
    SlopeTan["Compute tan theta equals dy by dx equals 4h over L squared times L minus 2x"]:::geomNode
    SinCos["Resolve sin theta and cos theta from tan theta"]:::geomNode
    Veff["Compute effective vertical force V effective equals VA minus W to the left"]:::forceNode
    BMom["Compute Bending Moment M sub x using M equals V effective times x minus H y minus load moments"]:::momentNode
    NThrust["Compute Normal Thrust N equals V effective sin theta plus H cos theta"]:::thrustNode
    RShear["Compute Radial Shear S equals V effective cos theta minus H sin theta"]:::shearNode
    CheckCrown["Check: M at crown x equals L over 2 must equal ZERO"]:::checkNode

    SecInput --> CoordY
    SecInput --> SlopeTan
    SlopeTan --> SinCos
    SecInput --> Veff
    Veff --> BMom
    Veff --> NThrust
    Veff --> RShear
    SinCos --> NThrust
    SinCos --> RShear
    BMom --> CheckCrown

    classDef secNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:2px
    classDef geomNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#1E3A5F,stroke-width:2px
    classDef forceNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef momentNode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    classDef thrustNode fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
    classDef shearNode fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef checkNode fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Geometry of a Three-Hinged Parabolic Arch

```
                Crown C (hinge)
                  /\
                 /  \  
                /    \  rise h
               /      \
              /        \
             /          \
            /            \  Tangent at section makes angle theta
           /              \ with the horizontal
          /                \  
         /                  \  
        A____________________B
              Span L
```

**Coordinate system:** Origin at A; x horizontal rightward; y vertical upward.
- Equation: $y = \frac{4h}{L^{2}} x (L - x)$
- Crown at $(L/2,\, h)$.
- Section at $x$ has $y$-coordinate $y(x)$ and tangent slope $\tan\theta = dy/dx$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **tangent to the arch** at any section is the local "axis" of the arch ring. The angle $\theta$ is measured from the horizontal. The **normal thrust $N$** acts along this tangent (pushing inward), and the **radial shear $S$** acts perpendicular to it. These are the quantities a designer uses to size the cross-section.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> **"Define a three-hinged arch. Show that the degree of static indeterminacy of a three-hinged arch is zero."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **three-hinged arch** is a curved structural member supported by two hinged supports at the abutments and provided with a third hinge (called the **crown hinge**) at the apex. It is used to transfer vertical loads primarily through axial compression to the abutments.

**DSI calculation:**

For a general plane curved beam: DSI = 3 (3 equilibrium equations, 4 unknowns $V_A, H_A, V_B, H_B$, so 1° indeterminate).

The **crown hinge** provides one additional condition equation: **$M_{\text{crown}} = 0$** (bending moment at the crown hinge is zero because the hinge cannot transmit moment).

$$\text{DSI} = (\text{reactions} + \text{condition equations}) - (\text{equilibrium equations}) = (4) - (3 + 1) = 0$$

Hence, a three-hinged arch is **statically determinate**. (3 marks)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> **"For a three-hinged parabolic arch carrying a UDL over the entire span, derive the expression for horizontal thrust."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a three-hinged parabolic arch of span $L$ and rise $h$, subjected to a UDL of intensity $w$ over the entire horizontal span:

By symmetry, $V_A = V_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$.

Apply the crown-hinge condition: $M_{\text{crown}} = 0$, considering the left half of the arch.

$$\sum M_{\text{crown}}^{\text{left}} = 0:\quad V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - w \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{4} = 0$$

$$\frac{wL^{2}}{4} - Hh - \frac{wL^{2}}{8} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Hh = \frac{wL^{2}}{8}$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{wL^{2}}{8h}} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Part B questions carry internal choice. Solve any ONE of the following.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> **A three-hinged parabolic arch has a span of 30 m and a central rise of 6 m. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over the left half of the span and a point load of 100 kN at a distance of 20 m from the left support. Determine:**
> **(a) The reactions at the supports, and (7 marks)**
> **(b) The bending moment, normal thrust, and radial shear at a section 10 m from the left support. (7 marks)**

#### **Solution (Model Answer):**

**Given:** $L = 30$ m, $h = 6$ m, UDL $w = 20$ kN/m on left half ($0 \le x \le 15$ m), point load $W = 100$ kN at $a = 20$ m from A.

**Geometry:** $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}} x(L - x) = \dfrac{4 \times 6}{900} x(30 - x) = \dfrac{x(30-x)}{37.5}$

At $x = 10$ m: $y = \dfrac{10 \times 20}{37.5} = \dfrac{200}{37.5} = 5.333$ m

$\tan\theta = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}(L - 2x) = \dfrac{24}{900}(30 - 20) = \dfrac{24 \times 10}{900} = 0.2667$

$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.2667) = 14.93^{\circ}$

$\sin\theta = 0.2577$, $\cos\theta = 0.9662$

**Part (a) — Reactions (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Total vertical loads.**
- UDL on left half: $20 \times 15 = 300$ kN
- Point load: $100$ kN
- Total vertical = $300 + 100 = 400$ kN

**Step 2: $V_A$ and $V_B$ from global equilibrium.**

Take moments about A:
$$V_B \cdot 30 - 300 \times 7.5 - 100 \times 20 = 0$$
$$V_B \cdot 30 = 2250 + 2000 = 4250$$
$$V_B = 141.67 \text{ kN}$$
$$V_A = 400 - 141.67 = 258.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: $H$ from crown-hinge condition.**

Apply $\sum M_{\text{crown}}^{\text{left}} = 0$ (crown at $x = 15$ m, $y = 6$ m):

Forces on left half:
- $V_A = 258.33$ kN (up) at A → moment about C: $+258.33 \times 15$ (CCW positive)
- $H$ (inward) at A → moment about C: $-H \times 6$ (CW)
- UDL total 300 kN at centroid $x = 7.5$ m, so $7.5$ m from C → moment: $-300 \times (15 - 7.5) = -300 \times 7.5 = -2250$ (CW, due to downward load on left of crown)
- Point load is at $x = 20$ m, which is on the **right half**, so it does **not** act on the left half. ✓

$$258.33 \times 15 - 6H - 2250 = 0$$
$$3875 - 6H - 2250 = 0$$
$$6H = 1625 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = 270.83 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (b) — Bending Moment, Normal Thrust, Radial Shear at x = 10 m (7 marks):**

Loads to the left of section ($x = 10$ m):
- $V_A = 258.33$ kN (upward at A)
- $H = 270.83$ kN (inward at A)
- UDL over $0$ to $10$ m = $20 \times 10 = 200$ kN (downward), centroid at $x = 5$ m

**Effective vertical force at section:**
$$V_{\text{eff}} = V_A - 200 = 258.33 - 200 = 58.33 \text{ kN (upward)}$$

**Bending moment at $x = 10$ m:**
$$M_x = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y - (\text{UDL moment about section})$$
$$M_{10} = 258.33 \times 10 - 270.83 \times 5.333 - 200 \times (10 - 5)$$
$$M_{10} = 2583.3 - 1444.5 - 1000 = \mathbf{138.8 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Normal thrust $N$:**
$$N = V_{\text{eff}} \sin\theta + H \cos\theta = 58.33 \times 0.2577 + 270.83 \times 0.9662$$
$$N = 15.03 + 261.65 = \mathbf{276.7 \text{ kN (compression)}}$$

**Radial shear $S$:**
$$S = V_{\text{eff}} \cos\theta - H \sin\theta = 58.33 \times 0.9662 - 270.83 \times 0.2577$$
$$S = 56.36 - 69.79 = \mathbf{-13.43 \text{ kN}}$$

(Negative sign indicates direction reversal; magnitude is 13.43 kN.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **A three-hinged circular arch has a span of 24 m and a rise of 4 m. It carries a concentrated load of 60 kN at a horizontal distance of 6 m from the left support. Determine:**
> **(a) The horizontal thrust and the reactions at the supports. (7 marks)**
> **(b) The maximum positive bending moment in the arch and the point where it occurs. (7 marks)**

#### **Solution (Model Answer):**

**Given:** Circular arch, $L = 24$ m, $h = 4$ m, point load $W = 60$ kN at $a = 6$ m from A.

**Geometry of Circular Arch:**

For a circular arch of span $L$ and rise $h$, the radius $R$ is:

$$R = \frac{L^{2}}{8h} + \frac{h}{2} = \frac{24^{2}}{32} + \frac{4}{2} = 18 + 2 = 20 \text{ m}$$

The central angle $2\alpha$ subtended at the centre is:

$$2\alpha = 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{L/2}{R}\right) = 2 \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{12}{20}\right) = 2 \times 36.87^{\circ} = 73.74^{\circ}$$

Crown is at angle $\alpha = 36.87^{\circ}$ from the vertical through the centre.

**Part (a) — Reactions and Horizontal Thrust (7 marks):**

**Vertical reactions:**
$$V_A = \frac{W(L-a)}{L} = \frac{60 \times 18}{24} = 45 \text{ kN}$$
$$V_B = \frac{Wa}{L} = \frac{60 \times 6}{24} = 15 \text{ kN}$$

**Horizontal Thrust:**

For a circular arch, the crown is at height $h = 4$ m above the springing line. The crown-hinge condition applied to the left half (load is at $a = 6 < L/2 = 12$, so it lies on the left half):

$$\sum M_{\text{crown}}^{\text{left}} = 0:\quad V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - W \cdot \left(\frac{L}{2} - a\right) = 0$$

$$45 \times 12 - H \times 4 - 60 \times (12 - 6) = 0$$
$$540 - 4H - 360 = 0$$
$$4H = 180 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{H = 45 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Positive Bending Moment (7 marks):**

Bending moment at a general section $x$ on the **left half** depends on whether the section is to the left or right of the load ($x < 6$ or $x > 6$).

**Case 1: $0 \le x \le 6$ m (section to the left of the load)**

No load between A and section except reactions. Moment of arch rib about the section (taking heights $y$ from the springing line for the circular profile — see below):

For the circular arch, the ordinate $y$ at horizontal distance $x$ from A is:

$$y = \sqrt{R^{2} - (R - h - x)^{2}} \quad \text{or via geometry: } y = R - R\cos\phi \text{ where } \phi = \text{angle from crown}$$

Using simpler expression for the circular arch with crown at $(L/2, h)$ and centre at $(L/2, h - R) = (12, -16)$:

$$y(x) = -16 + \sqrt{20^{2} - (x - 12)^{2}}$$

At $x = 6$ m: $y = -16 + \sqrt{400 - 36} = -16 + \sqrt{364} = -16 + 19.079 = 3.079$ m

Bending moment for $x \le 6$:
$$M(x) = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y(x) = 45x - 45 y(x)$$

**Case 2: $6 \le x \le 12$ m (section between the load and the crown)**

Now the load is to the left of the section, contributing an additional moment $-W(x-a)$ (load creates a "sagging" contribution if we are to its right):

$$M(x) = V_A \cdot x - H \cdot y(x) - W(x - a) = 45x - 45 y(x) - 60(x - 6)$$

**Finding Maximum Bending Moment:**

Differentiate $M(x)$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero.

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V_A - H \frac{dy}{dx} - \text{(load contribution)}$$

For Case 1 ($x \le 6$):
$$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{-(x - 12)}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}} = \frac{12 - x}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}}$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = 45 - 45 \cdot \frac{12 - x}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}} = 0$$

$$\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}} = 12 - x$$

Squaring: $400 - (x-12)^{2} = (12-x)^{2} = (x-12)^{2}$

$$400 = 2(x-12)^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad (x-12)^{2} = 200 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x - 12 = -14.14$$

$$x = -2.14 \text{ m (Not in domain)} \quad \text{or} \quad x = 26.14 \text{ m (out of range)}$$

**Conclusion from Case 1:** No stationary point in $0 \le x \le 6$. Maximum at boundary $x = 6$.

$$M(6) = 45 \times 6 - 45 \times 3.079 = 270 - 138.55 = 131.45 \text{ kN·m}$$

For Case 2 ($6 \le x \le 12$):
$$\frac{dM}{dx} = 45 - 60 - 45 \cdot \frac{12 - x}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}} = 0$$

$$-15 = 45 \cdot \frac{12 - x}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}}$$

$$\frac{12 - x}{\sqrt{400 - (x-12)^{2}}} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

This requires $x > 12$, which is outside the left-half domain. So **no interior maximum in Case 2 either.**

**Hence, the maximum positive bending moment occurs at the load point $x = 6$ m:**

$$\boxed{M_{\max}^{+} = 131.45 \text{ kN·m at } x = 6 \text{ m}}$$

(Equivalent to finding where the **shear force changes sign** — the conventional method for finding $M_{\max}$.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones (Examiner's Notes):**
> 1. **Forgetting the crown-hinge condition:** Many students apply only the 3 global equilibrium equations to the whole arch, get 3 unknowns (and 1 indeterminate left over), and then "guess" $H$ by analogy. **You will lose 3–4 marks** for not using $\sum M_{\text{crown}} = 0$ explicitly.
> 2. **Sign convention for $H$:** The horizontal thrust acts **inward** at both supports. Drawing it outward will flip the sign of $H$ in the moment equation, leading to wrong moment values throughout.
> 3. **Mixing $x$ and arc-length $s$:** The arch is described by **horizontal** coordinate $x$, not arc length. The parabola equation $y = 4hx(L-x)/L^2$ is in $x$, not in $s$. Forgetting this and writing $y = 4hs(L-s)/L^2$ is a **fatal error**.
> 4. **Unit confusion in $H = wL^2/(8h)$:** $H$ comes out in kN only if $w$ is in kN/m, $L$ in m, and $h$ in m. Mixing units (e.g., $L$ in cm) loses full marks.
> 5. **Neglecting "to-the-left-of-section" loads in BM equation:** A common error is to write $M_x = V_A x - H y$ everywhere. This is only valid for sections **to the left of all loads**. Once the section passes a load, the load's moment contribution must be subtracted. Exam invigilators specifically look for this.
> 6. **Circular vs parabolic mix-up:** The horizontal thrust formula $H = wL^2/(8h)$ is **strictly for parabolic arches under full-span UDL**. For circular arches or other loadings, you must derive $H$ from the crown-hinge condition explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Three-Hinged Arches:**
>
> ✅ **DSI = 0** — three hinges (2 supports + 1 crown) make the arch statically determinate.
>
> ✅ **The crown-hinge condition** $\sum M_{\text{crown}} = 0$ is the "fourth equation" that closes the system.
>
> ✅ **Geometry of parabolic arch:** $y = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}} x(L - x)$, with crown at $(L/2, h)$.
>
> ✅ **Slope of tangent:** $\tan\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{4h}{L^{2}}(L - 2x)$.
>
> ✅ **Universal formulas to memorize (parabolic, point load $W$ at $a$):**
> - $V_A = W(L-a)/L$, $V_B = Wa/L$
> - $H = Wa(L-a)/(Lh)$
>
> ✅ **Universal formulas to memorize (parabolic, full-span UDL $w$):**
> - $V_A = V_B = wL/2$
> - $H = wL^{2}/(8h)$
>
> ✅ **Bending moment at section:** $M_x = V_A x - Hy - (\text{load moments left of section})$.
>
> ✅ **Normal thrust and radial shear:**
> $N = V_{\text{eff}}\sin\theta + H\cos\theta$, $\quad S = V_{\text{eff}}\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$.
>
> ✅ **Temperature effect:** $H_t = 15 \alpha T EI / (8 h^{2})$ (parabolic, uniform $EI$).
>
> ✅ **Settlement effect:** $H_s = 15 EI \delta / (2 L h^{2})$ (vertical settlement of one support).
>
> ✅ **Horizontal thrust is constant** along the entire arch for vertical loads.
>
> ✅ **$H \propto 1/h$:** Flat arches (small $h$) carry very large horizontal thrust — heavy abutments required.
>
> ✅ **Crown moment is always zero** — use this as a verification check after computing any $M_x$.
>
> ✅ **Influence line for $H$** (parabolic arch): $H$-line for a unit load at distance $u$ from A is $H(u) = u(L-u)/(Lh)$, a parabola in $u$ with maximum $L/(4h)$ at $u = L/2$.
>
> ✅ **Influence line ordinates for vertical reactions** are identical to a simply-supported beam (since $V_A, V_B$ depend only on global vertical equilibrium).
>
> ✅ **Maximum BM location** is found where the **shear force changes sign** — not at midspan in general.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal Thrust and Radial Shear Due to Simple Cases of Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVT303 - ANALYSIS OF STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal Thrust and Radial Shear Due to Simple Cases of Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the structural analysis of **arches**, any cross-section of the arch is subjected to three internal resultants when a general system of loads acts on it. Two of the most important are:

- **Normal Thrust (N):** The resultant internal force acting *tangential* to the centerline (axis) of the arch at the section. It represents the compressive (or tensile) force along the curved axis of the arch.
- **Radial Shear (S):** The resultant internal force acting *perpendicular* to the centerline of the arch at the section. It represents the shear force resolved along the radial direction of the arch.

The third internal resultant is the **Bending Moment (M)**, acting in the plane of the arch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Normal Thrust and Radial Shear?**
> Unlike a straight beam where shear and axial force are taken as **Vertical Shear (V)** and **Axial Thrust (H)**, the curved axis of an arch makes these global directions meaningless at a typical section. Therefore, forces are resolved along the **local axes** of the arch — tangential (normal thrust) and radial (radial shear) — to correctly understand the design forces on the masonry/concrete section.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Skier on a Curved Slope

Imagine a skier sliding down a curved hill. At any point on the slope:

- The component of gravity **along the slope** (tangent direction) tries to either push the skier down the hill or hold them back — this is analogous to the **Normal Thrust** (a force along the arch axis).
- The component **into the slope** (perpendicular to the path) presses the skier into the snow — this is analogous to the **Radial Shear**.

The same logic applies to an arch: the global vertical load (gravity) and the horizontal thrust from supports are resolved into the arch's local tangential and radial directions, giving us **N** and **S** at the section.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Sign Convention

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Thrust | **N** | **kN** or **N** |
| Radial Shear | **S** | **kN** or **N** |
| Inclination of tangent | **θ** | **radians** (or degrees) |
| Horizontal Thrust | **H** | **kN** |
| Vertical Reaction/Shear | **V** | **kN** |
| Span of arch | **L** | **m** |
| Central rise | **h** | **m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Normal Thrust (N):** Always assumed **compressive** and taken as **positive** (since arches carry loads primarily through compression).
> - **Radial Shear (S):** Positive when it acts **inward** toward the center of curvature (i.e., from outer fiber toward the arch axis).
> - **Bending Moment (M):** Positive when it causes **tension on the inner (concave) fiber** of the arch.

## 1.4 General Resolution of Forces at an Arch Section

Consider a section at an angle **θ** with the horizontal, with internal force components:
- **H** = horizontal force (thrust) at the section
- **V** = vertical force (shear) at the section

The resolution gives:

$$N = H \cos\theta + V \sin\theta$$

$$S = V \cos\theta - H \sin\theta$$

These are the **two master equations** for the topic and must be memorized by every KTU student.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Force Resolution Geometry — The "Why" Behind the Formula

At any point on the arch, the arch axis makes an angle **θ** with the horizontal. The local coordinate system at that point is:
- **Tangential direction (t):** Along the arch axis (where N acts)
- **Radial direction (r):** Perpendicular to the arch axis (where S acts)

The global forces **H** (horizontal) and **V** (vertical) at the section must be rotated by **θ** to align with the local axes. The rotation matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} N \\ S \end{bmatrix} = 
\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} H \\ V \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a standard 2D rotation by angle θ. The physical meaning:
- **H** projected onto the tangent direction = **H cos θ** (contributes to N)
- **H** projected onto the radial direction = **H sin θ** (contributes to S)
- **V** projected onto the tangent direction = **V sin θ** (contributes to N)
- **V** projected onto the radial direction = **V cos θ** (contributes to S)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic for Analysis of Arches

1. **Step 1 — Determine the geometry:** Identify span **L**, central rise **h**, and the type of arch (circular, parabolic, parabolic equivalent of circular).
2. **Step 2 — Calculate the support reactions:**
   - For a **3-hinged arch** (statically determinate): Use the central hinge condition (M = 0 at the crown) to find **H** (horizontal thrust).
   - For a **2-hinged arch** (statically indeterminate to 1°): Use elastic methods (e.g., Castigliano's theorem, elastic center method).
3. **Step 3 — Choose a section at angle θ:** From geometry, express **tan θ = dy/dx** at that section.
4. **Step 4 — Compute H and V at the section:**
   - **H** = horizontal thrust (constant throughout for vertical loads)
   - **V** = total vertical reaction – sum of vertical loads between support and section
5. **Step 5 — Apply the master equations:** Compute **N** and **S** using the formulas in Section 1.4.

## 2.3 Simple Cases of Loading (KTU High-Yield Cases)

### Case 1: Point Load (W) at a Specific Location

For a 3-hinged arch, the load travels to the nearest support, and the horizontal thrust is found using the central hinge condition.

### Case 2: UDL Over the Entire Span (Most Common in KTU)

For a **3-hinged parabolic arch** with UDL **w** per unit length over the full span:

$$H = \frac{wL^2}{8h}$$

At a section at horizontal distance **x** from the support:
- Vertical shear from reactions: **V = wL/2 – wx = w(L/2 – x)**
- For parabolic arch: **tan θ = 4h(L – 2x)/L²**

### Case 3: UDL Over Part of the Span

Requires careful calculation of vertical loads on each side of the section, with H computed from the central hinge moment condition.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Thrust | $N = H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta$ | kN | Compression is +ve |
| 2 | Radial Shear | $S = V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$ | kN | Inward radial is +ve |
| 3 | Bending Moment | $M = Vx - Hy - M_{loads}$ | kN·m | Standard convention |
| 4 | 3-hinged arch H (full UDL) | $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$ | kN | For parabolic arch |
| 5 | 3-hinged arch H (point load) | $H = \dfrac{W \cdot a \cdot (L-a)}{Lh}$ | kN | Load at distance $a$ from support |
| 6 | Slope of parabolic arch | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{4h(L-2x)}{L^2}$ | — | $x$ from left support |
| 7 | Slope of circular arch | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{L-2x}{2h - \sqrt{4h^2-(L-2x)^2}\cdot\tfrac{2}{L}}$ | — | Geometry dependent |
| 8 | Radius of circular arch | $R = \dfrac{L^2/4 + h^2}{2h}$ | m | Standard formula |
| 9 | θ at supports (parabolic) | $\tan\theta_s = \dfrac{4h}{L}$ | — | Tangent slope at ends |
| 10 | θ at crown | $\theta_c = 0$ | — | Tangent is horizontal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Normal thrust and radial shear values are essential for:
> - **Design of masonry/concrete arches** — checking compressive stress = N/A
> - **Reinforcement design** — S and M determine shear and flexural steel
> - **Bridge arch analysis** — critical for spans exceeding 30 m
> - **Dam and tunnel arches** — extreme compressive loads
> - **Historical structure assessment** — retrofitting medieval arches

## 2.5 Circular vs. Parabolic Arches — Key Distinctions

| Feature | Circular Arch | Parabolic Arch |
|---|---|---|
| Shape equation | $(x-L/2)^2 + (y-h)^2 = R^2$ | $y = 4hx(L-x)/L^2$ |
| Natural loading | Hydrostatic pressure | UDL over full span |
| H under UDL | $H = wL^2/(8h)$ (approx.) | $H = wL^2/(8h)$ (exact) |
| Slope expression | Complex trigonometric | Simple polynomial |
| Use case | Aesthetic / small spans | Bridges, long spans |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Normal Thrust and Radial Shear Master Equations

### Setup

Consider an arch with the left support at origin (0, 0) and the right support at (L, 0). The crown is at (L/2, h). Take a section at horizontal distance **x** from the left support, where the tangent to the arch makes angle **θ** with the horizontal.

The forces acting on the **left portion** of the arch (from support to section) are:
- **Reaction H_A** (horizontal thrust) acting inward at the support
- **Reaction V_A** (vertical) acting upward
- **All applied loads** on the left segment

The internal forces at the section, resolved along global axes, are:
- **H** = horizontal force at the section (positive = pushing outward on the left portion)
- **V** = vertical force at the section (positive = acting upward on the left portion)

### Free Body Diagram of Left Portion

The left portion is in equilibrium. The internal forces **H** and **V** at the cut section balance the external reactions and loads.

### Resolution to Local Axes (The Critical Step)

At the cut section, define a **local coordinate system**:
- **t-direction**: along the tangent, pointing in the direction of increasing arc length (toward the right support)
- **r-direction**: perpendicular to t, pointing toward the **center of curvature** (inward)

The transformation from global (H, V) to local (N, S) requires a rotation by angle **θ**.

**Component of H along t (tangential):** The vector **H** is horizontal. Its component along the tangent (which is inclined at θ to horizontal) is:

$$H_t = H \cos\theta$$

This is the contribution of H to Normal Thrust.

**Component of H along r (radial):**

$$H_r = H \sin\theta$$

This is the contribution of H to Radial Shear.

**Component of V along t (tangential):** The vector **V** is vertical. The tangent is at angle θ. The angle between V (vertical) and t (tangent) is **(90° – θ)**. So:

$$V_t = V \cos(90° - \theta) = V \sin\theta$$

**Component of V along r (radial):**

$$V_r = V \cos\theta$$

### Summing Components

Normal Thrust = Component of H along t + Component of V along t

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

Radial Shear = Component of V along r − Component of H along r

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in the shear equation arises because **H** tends to push the section **outward** (away from the center of curvature), which is opposite to the positive radial direction (inward).

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: Horizontal Thrust for UDL on a 3-Hinged Parabolic Arch

### Given
- 3-hinged parabolic arch with span **L**, rise **h**
- UDL of intensity **w** over the entire horizontal span
- Hinges at both supports and the crown

### Step 1: Support Reactions by Symmetry

By symmetry of geometry and loading:
- **V_A = V_B = wL/2**
- **H_A = H_B = H** (horizontal thrust, to be found)

### Step 2: Apply the Central Hinge Condition

At the crown (x = L/2, y = h), the bending moment is **zero** because the central hinge cannot transmit moment.

Take the left half of the arch. Sum moments about the crown (taken as the point where the moment is zero):

Forces on the left half:
- V_A = wL/2 (upward at left support)
- H_A = H (inward at left support)
- UDL = w over length L/2

Moments about the crown (taking clockwise as positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{crown} &= V_A \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - w \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{4} \\
&= \frac{wL}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} - H \cdot h - w \cdot \frac{L^2}{8} \\
&= \frac{wL^2}{4} - Hh - \frac{wL^2}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Set M_crown = 0 (Hinge Condition)

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= \frac{wL^2}{4} - Hh - \frac{wL^2}{8} \\
Hh &= \frac{wL^2}{4} - \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{wL^2}{8} \\
H &= \frac{wL^2}{8h}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **famous horizontal thrust formula** for a 3-hinged parabolic arch under full UDL.

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

### Problem Statement

A 3-hinged parabolic arch has a span of **L = 20 m** and a central rise of **h = 4 m**. It carries a UDL of **w = 10 kN/m** over the entire span. Calculate the **Normal Thrust** and **Radial Shear** at a section **4 m** from the left support.

### Step 1: Compute Reactions

By symmetry:
- **V_A = V_B = wL/2 = 10 × 20 / 2 = 100 kN**
- **H = wL²/(8h) = 10 × 400 / (8 × 4) = 5000 / 32 = 156.25 kN**

### Step 2: Compute Geometry at x = 4 m

For a parabolic arch: $y = \dfrac{4hx(L-x)}{L^2} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 4 \cdot x \cdot (20-x)}{400} = \dfrac{16x(20-x)}{400}$

At x = 4 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{16 \cdot 4 \cdot 16}{400} = \frac{1024}{400} = 2.56 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Slope of the tangent at the section:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dy}{dx} &= \frac{4h(L - 2x)}{L^2} = \frac{4 \cdot 4 \cdot (20 - 8)}{400} = \frac{16 \cdot 12}{400} = \frac{192}{400} = 0.48 \\
\tan\theta &= 0.48 \\
\theta &= \tan^{-1}(0.48) = 25.64°
\end{aligned}
$$

So: $\cos\theta = 0.8862$ and $\sin\theta = 0.4633$

### Step 3: Compute Vertical Shear V at the Section

The section is 4 m from the left support. The UDL on the left segment is **w × 4 = 10 × 4 = 40 kN** acting at 2 m from the section.

Vertical equilibrium of left portion (taking upward forces as positive):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= V_A - w \cdot x = 100 - 10 \cdot 4 = 100 - 40 = 60 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute Normal Thrust N

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta \\
&= 156.25 \cdot 0.8862 + 60 \cdot 0.4633 \\
&= 138.47 + 27.80 \\
&= 166.27 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Radial Shear S

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta \\
&= 60 \cdot 0.8862 - 156.25 \cdot 0.4633 \\
&= 53.17 - 72.39 \\
&= -19.22 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result Summary

| Quantity | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Thrust N** | **+166.27 kN** | Compressive, safe for masonry |
| **Radial Shear S** | **−19.22 kN** | Outward radial (negative) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign for S indicates the radial shear acts **outward** at this section — a critical detail for reinforcement design. In KTU exams, **always include the sign** with proper justification.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Edge Behavior of N and S

| Location | x (m) | θ | H (kN) | V (kN) | N (kN) | S (kN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Left support | 0 | 21.8° | 156.25 | 100 | 184.9 | 30.4 |
| Quarter span | 5 | 14.0° | 156.25 | 50 | 173.6 | −6.2 |
| Crown | 10 | 0° | 156.25 | 0 | 156.25 | 0 |
| Three-quarter span | 15 | 14.0° | 156.25 | −50 | 138.9 | −36.6 |
| Right support | 20 | 21.8° | 156.25 | −100 | 108.4 | −123.4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** N is **maximum at the supports** (where θ is largest) and **minimum at the crown** (where θ = 0, N = H). S is **zero at the crown** and generally small compared to N in well-designed parabolic arches under UDL.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Analysis Workflow for N and S in Arches

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Arch Geometry and Loading] --> B[Identify Arch Type: 3-Hinged / 2-Hinged / Fixed]
    B --> C[Calculate Vertical Reactions V_A and V_B]
    C --> D[Calculate Horizontal Thrust H]
    D --> D1{3-Hinged? Use M_crown=0}
    D --> D2{2-Hinged? Use Elastic Method}
    D1 --> E[Select Section at Distance x from Left Support]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Slope dy/dx at x]
    F --> G[Find Angle theta = tan⁻¹ dy/dx]
    G --> H[Compute V at Section from Equilibrium]
    H --> I[Apply Master Equations]
    I --> J[N = H cos theta + V sin theta]
    I --> K[S = V cos theta - H sin theta]
    J --> L[Output: Normal Thrust and Radial Shear]
    K --> L
    L --> M[End: Design Forces Available]
```

## 4.2 Free Body Diagram Schematic — Section Forces on a 3-Hinged Arch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[Left Portion of Arch]
        SA[Support A: V_A up, H_A inward] --> SECTION[Section at angle theta]
        LOAD[UDL w over length x] --> SECTION
    end
    subgraph FORCES[Internal Forces at Section]
        SECTION --> H[Horizontal H]
        SECTION --> V[Vertical V]
    end
    subgraph LOCAL[Local Resolution]
        H --> NCOS[H cos theta contributes to N]
        H --> HSIN[H sin theta contributes to S]
        V --> VSIN[V sin theta contributes to N]
        V --> VCOS[V cos theta contributes to S]
        NCOS --> N[Normal Thrust N]
        VSIN --> N
        VCOS --> S[Radial Shear S]
        HSIN --> S
    end
```

## 4.3 Force Resolution Block Diagram — Global to Local Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GLOBAL[Global Forces H and V at Section] --> ROT[Rotation by Angle theta]
    ROT --> TANG[Tangential Direction: N = H cos theta + V sin theta]
    ROT --> RAD[Radial Direction: S = V cos theta - H sin theta]
    TANG --> CHECK1{Compression Check}
    CHECK1 -->|Yes| DESIGN_OK[Section Safe in Compression]
    CHECK1 -->|No| CRITICAL[Section Critical - Redesign]
    RAD --> CHECK2{Shear Magnitude Check}
    CHECK2 -->|Within Limits| DESIGN_OK
    CHECK2 -->|Exceeds Limit| CRITICAL
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Loading Cases vs. Formula Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Simple Loading Cases] --> L1[Point Load at Distance a]
    LOAD --> L2[UDL over Full Span]
    LOAD --> L3[UDL over Part of Span]
    L1 --> F1[H = W a L-a over Lh]
    L2 --> F2[H = w L squared over 8h]
    L3 --> F3[Use Moment about Crown]
    F1 --> COMPUTE[Compute N and S using Master Equations]
    F2 --> COMPUTE
    F3 --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> OUTPUT[Final N and S Values at Required Section]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These block diagrams map the analytical workflow. In an actual KTU exam, students are expected to draw a **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** of the left/right portion showing V, H, and the applied loads, with the local N and S vectors resolved at the section.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

Define **Normal Thrust** and **Radial Shear** in the context of arch analysis. State the equations relating them to horizontal and vertical forces at a section.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Normal Thrust (N):** The internal force acting tangentially to the centerline of the arch at a given section. It represents the axial (compressive) force along the curved axis of the arch. **(1 mark)**

- **Radial Shear (S):** The internal force acting perpendicular to the centerline of the arch at a given section. It represents the shear force resolved along the radial direction of the arch. **(1 mark)**

- **Equations:** If **H** and **V** are the horizontal and vertical forces at a section inclined at angle **θ** to the horizontal: **(1 mark)**

$$N = H\cos\theta + V\sin\theta$$
$$S = V\cos\theta - H\sin\theta$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]**

A 3-hinged parabolic arch has a span of 16 m and a central rise of 4 m. A UDL of 5 kN/m acts over the entire span. Calculate the horizontal thrust.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- Span: L = 16 m, Rise: h = 4 m, UDL: w = 5 kN/m
- Formula for horizontal thrust under full UDL: $H = \dfrac{wL^2}{8h}$ **(1 mark)**
- Substitution: $H = \dfrac{5 \times 16^2}{8 \times 4} = \dfrac{5 \times 256}{32} = \dfrac{1280}{32}$ **(1 mark)**
- **Final Answer: H = 40 kN** **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, End Semester Examination]**

A 3-hinged circular arch has a span of **30 m** and a central rise of **5 m**. A point load of **120 kN** acts at a distance of **10 m** from the left support. Calculate:
- **(a)** The horizontal thrust and vertical reactions. **(7 marks)**
- **(b)** The normal thrust and radial shear at the section just under the load. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Reactions and Horizontal Thrust (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Geometry of the circular arch** (1 mark)
- Span L = 30 m, Rise h = 5 m
- Radius: $R = \dfrac{L^2/4 + h^2}{2h} = \dfrac{225 + 25}{10} = \dfrac{250}{10} = 25$ m

**Step 2: Vertical reactions** (2 marks)

Let V_A and V_B be the vertical reactions at A and B.
Taking moments about B:
$$V_A \cdot 30 - 120 \cdot 20 = 0$$
$$V_A = \dfrac{120 \times 20}{30} = 80 \text{ kN}$$

By vertical equilibrium: V_B = 120 − 80 = 40 kN

**Step 3: Horizontal thrust using central hinge** (4 marks)

At the crown (x = 15 m, y = 5 m), M = 0. Taking moments of all forces to the left of the crown:

$$V_A \cdot 15 - H \cdot 5 - 120 \cdot 5 = 0$$
$$80 \cdot 15 - 5H - 600 = 0$$
$$1200 - 5H - 600 = 0$$
$$5H = 600$$
$$\boxed{H = 120 \text{ kN}}$$

#### Part (b) Model Solution — N and S at x = 10 m (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Find slope angle θ at x = 10 m** (2 marks)

For a circular arch with center at (15, 5−25) = (15, −20):
- Distance from center to point (10, y): $\sqrt{(10-15)^2 + (y+20)^2} = 25$
